questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following correctly defines a mole?

choices.text: The mass in grams of one atom of an element
choices.explanation: One atom's mass in grams is called an atomic mass unit (amu), not a mole. A mole is $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ particles of any substance.
A mole is defined as exactly $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ elementary entities (atoms, molecules, ions, etc.). This allows conversion between mass and number of particles.

choices.text: The volume occupied by a gas at STP
choices.explanation: 22.4 L is the molar volume of an ideal gas at STP (273 K, 1 atm), not the definition of a mole. A mole is defined by the number of particles, not a volume.

choices.text: The number of neutrons in one gram of hydrogen
choices.explanation: This is a common misconception. One gram of hydrogen contains $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ atoms (1 mole), but a mole is defined as Avogadro's number of particles, not neutrons.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: A sample of iron has a mass of 55.85 g. The molar mass of iron is 55.85 g/mol. How many moles of iron are in the sample?

choices.explanation: 0.5 mol would require dividing 55.85 g by 111.7 g/mol, which is not the molar mass of iron.
$n = m/M = 55.85\ \text{g} / 55.85\ \text{g/mol} = 1.0\ \text{mol}$. The mass equals the molar mass, so there is exactly 1 mole.
55.85 mol would require a sample of 55.85 × 55.85 ≈ 3119 g of iron. Dividing mass by molar mass gives moles: 55.85/55.85 = 1.0 mol.
2.0 mol of iron would have mass 2 × 55.85 = 111.7 g. The sample is only 55.85 g, which equals exactly 1.0 mol.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: The mass spectrum shown is for a pure element. What is the average atomic mass of this element?

choices.explanation: 63.0 u would be the average if only the lighter isotope existed (100% abundance). The weighted average must account for the 31% abundance of the m/z = 65 isotope.
64.0 u is the simple arithmetic average of 63 and 65. Weighted average requires multiplying each mass by its fractional abundance: $(0.69)(63) + (0.31)(65) = 43.47 + 20.15 = 63.62$ u.
Weighted average atomic mass = $(0.69)(63) + (0.31)(65) = 43.47 + 20.15 = 63.62$ u. This is consistent with copper (63.55 u) which has two naturally occurring isotopes.
65.0 u would be the average if only the heavier isotope existed. Since the lighter isotope (m/z = 63) has 69% abundance, it dominates and the average is closer to 63.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Two atoms of the same element have atomic numbers of 17 but mass numbers of 35 and 37. These atoms are best described as

choices.explanation: Allotropes are different structural forms of the same element (e.g., diamond and graphite). Atoms with the same atomic number but different mass numbers are isotopes.

Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same number of protons, so same atomic number) with different mass numbers (different number of neutrons). Both atoms are chlorine with 17 protons but different neutron counts.
Isobars are atoms of different elements with the same mass number. These atoms have different mass numbers (35 and 37) and the same atomic number — they are isotopes.
Ions are atoms that have gained or lost electrons. These neutral atoms differ only in neutron count, making them isotopes, not ions.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: A compound is found to contain 40.0% carbon, 6.7% hydrogen, and 53.3% oxygen by mass. What is the empirical formula?

choices.explanation: $C_2H_4O_2$ has the same elemental ratio as $CH_2O$ but is not the simplest whole-number ratio. The empirical formula must be the simplest ratio: $CH_2O$.
Converting: C = 40/12 = 3.33, H = 6.7/1 = 6.7, O = 53.3/16 = 3.33. Dividing by 3.33: C:H:O = 1:2:1. The empirical formula is $CH_2O$, not $CHO$.
Moles: C = 40.0/12.0 = 3.33; H = 6.7/1.0 = 6.7; O = 53.3/16.0 = 3.33. Dividing by smallest (3.33): C:H:O = 1:2:1. Empirical formula = $CH_2O$.
$CH_3O$ would require H:C = 3:1. The calculation gives H:C = 6.7:3.33 ≈ 2:1, so the formula is $CH_2O$, not $CH_3O$.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following electron configurations represents a ground-state atom in the third period?

choices.explanation: This configuration has 16 electrons total (2+2+6+2+4 = 16), corresponding to sulfur (S, Z=16), a Period 3 element. All subshells are filled in Aufbau order.
Period 3 elements fill up to 3p subshell. $3d$ orbitals belong to Period 4 (4th shell fills d orbitals). This configuration would belong to a transition metal in Period 4.
2p subshell can hold a maximum of 6 electrons, not 8. This is an invalid electron configuration.
This configuration has 19 electrons corresponding to potassium (K), which is a Period 4 element (its outermost electron is in n=4). This is not a Period 3 configuration.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

title.text: Photoelectron Spectrum of Unknown Element X

questions.question: A photoelectron spectrum for element X shows three peaks with relative electron counts of 2 (highest binding energy), 8 (medium binding energy), and 1 (lowest binding energy). Which element is most consistent with this spectrum?

choices.explanation: Neon has 10 electrons with configuration $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$. Its PES would show peaks with counts of 2, 2, and 6 — not 2, 8, and 1.
Sodium has 11 electrons: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^1$. PES peaks: 2 electrons (highest BE, $1s^2$); 8 electrons (medium BE, $2s^2 + 2p^6$); 1 electron (lowest BE, $3s^1$). Matches 2, 8, 1.
Magnesium has configuration $[Ne]3s^2$. PES would show 2, 8, 2 — three peaks with the lowest-BE peak having 2 electrons, not 1.
Lithium has only 3 electrons: $1s^2 2s^1$. Its PES would show only two peaks (counts 2 and 1), not three peaks.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following correctly ranks the elements Li, C, and Ne in order of increasing first ionization energy?

choices.explanation: This is the reverse of the correct order. Neon has the highest ionization energy in Period 2 due to its large nuclear charge and complete valence shell.

First ionization energy generally increases across a period due to increasing nuclear charge with similar shielding. Li (Z=3) < C (Z=6) < Ne (Z=10). Note: O is slightly less than N due to electron pairing, but Li < C < Ne holds.
Carbon (Z=6) has a higher ionization energy than lithium (Z=3) because of greater nuclear charge and higher effective nuclear charge across the period.
Neon (Z=10) has higher ionization energy than carbon (Z=6) because it has more protons with similar shielding, and its full valence shell is particularly stable.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Moving from left to right across Period 3, which of the following properties shows a DECREASING trend?

choices.explanation: Electronegativity increases across a period because nuclear charge increases while shielding stays approximately constant, increasing the attraction for bonding electrons.
First ionization energy generally increases across a period (with minor exceptions) because higher nuclear charge holds valence electrons more tightly.

Atomic radius decreases across a period. As nuclear charge increases with similar electron shielding, electrons are pulled closer to the nucleus, reducing the radius. Na has the largest radius; Ar has the smallest in Period 3.
Effective nuclear charge increases across a period as more protons are added without proportionally more shielding from core electrons.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: The table shows first and second ionization energies for four Period 3 elements. Which element shows the most dramatic jump between the first and second ionization energies, and what best explains this?

choices.text: Magnesium; it has an unusually stable configuration after losing 2 electrons
choices.explanation: Magnesium shows a jump (738 to 1451 kJ/mol) but not the most dramatic. Sodium shows the largest jump from 1st IE (496) to 2nd IE (4562) — a factor of ~9.

choices.text: Sodium; after losing its single valence electron, the 2nd electron must come from a filled core shell
choices.explanation: Na has one 3s valence electron (IE₁ = 496). The 2nd electron would come from the 2p core shell, which is much closer to the nucleus with far less shielding. This dramatic jump (496 → 4562) identifies Na as having one valence electron.

choices.text: Silicon; it has the highest 1st IE in the table
choices.explanation: Silicon has the highest 1st IE (786 kJ/mol) in this table, but the relative jump from 1st to 2nd IE is not the largest. Sodium's 10-fold jump is far more dramatic.

choices.text: Aluminum; removing the 2nd electron disrupts its $3s^2$ configuration
choices.explanation: After losing 1 electron, Al has $[Ne]3s^2$ configuration. The 2nd IE of Al (1817) is not dramatically higher than the 1st (577) compared to Na's jump. Sodium's jump is the most dramatic.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: An element has the electron configuration $[Ne]3s^2 3p^3$. How many valence electrons does it have, and what charge would its most common ion have?

choices.text: 3 valence electrons; most commonly forms a 3+ ion
choices.explanation: 5 electrons (not 3) occupy the n=3 shell: $3s^2 3p^3$. This is phosphorus (P). Phosphorus more commonly gains 3 electrons to achieve an octet (P³⁻), rather than losing 5.

choices.text: 5 valence electrons; most commonly forms a 3− ion
choices.explanation: Valence electrons are in n=3: $3s^2 + 3p^3 = 5$ electrons. Phosphorus (P) needs 3 more electrons to complete its octet, forming P³⁻ in ionic compounds.

choices.text: 5 valence electrons; most commonly forms a 5+ ion
choices.explanation: While P can form +5 oxidation states in covalent compounds, as an ionic species P more commonly gains 3 electrons (P³⁻) rather than losing all 5 valence electrons.
The total valence electrons in n=3 are $3s^2 + 3p^3 = 5$. Only counting 3s electrons (2) would miss the 3p electrons that are also valence electrons.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following best explains why the first ionization energy of oxygen (Z=8) is slightly lower than that of nitrogen (Z=7), despite oxygen having a higher nuclear charge?

choices.text: Oxygen has more electrons than nitrogen, increasing electron-electron repulsion overall
choices.explanation: While oxygen has more total electrons, the specific reason for the exception is about orbital occupancy in 2p, not general electron count.

choices.text: Nitrogen has a half-filled 2p subshell that is unusually stable due to exchange energy
choices.explanation: Nitrogen's $2p^3$ configuration places one electron in each 2p orbital (Hund's rule). This half-filled arrangement has extra stability from electron exchange interactions. In oxygen, two electrons must share a 2p orbital, experiencing extra repulsion — making the second electron in that orbital easier to remove.

choices.text: Oxygen's electrons are in higher-energy orbitals than nitrogen's
choices.explanation: Both N and O have valence electrons in 2p orbitals — the same subshell. The exception occurs because of electron pairing within a 2p orbital in oxygen, not because of different subshells.

choices.text: Oxygen atoms are larger than nitrogen atoms, so valence electrons are held less tightly
choices.explanation: Oxygen atoms are actually smaller than nitrogen atoms (atomic radius decreases across a period). The correct explanation involves electron pairing repulsion in oxygen's 2p subshell.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: A sample of water ($H_2O$, molar mass 18 g/mol) has a mass of 36 g. How many molecules of water are in this sample?

choices.explanation: This would be 1 mole. 36 g / 18 g·mol⁻¹ = 2 moles. Two moles contains $2 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ molecules.
$n = 36/18 = 2\ \text{mol}$. Number of molecules = $2 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 1.204 \times 10^{24}$ molecules.
This is $0.5 \times N_A$, which would correspond to 0.5 mol (9 g of water). The sample has 36 g = 2 mol = $1.204 \times 10^{24}$ molecules.
2.0 is the number of moles, not molecules. To convert moles to molecules, multiply by Avogadro's number: $2 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 1.204 \times 10^{24}$ molecules.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following electron configurations is NOT correct for a ground-state atom?

choices.explanation: This is the correct ground-state configuration for magnesium (Mg, Z=12). Electrons fill according to Aufbau principle.
This is the correct ground-state configuration for fluorine (F, Z=9).
This configuration skips filling the 3s subshell before 3p. The Aufbau principle requires 3s to be filled before 3p. The correct configuration for 11 electrons (Na) is $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^1$.
This is the correct ground-state configuration for nitrogen (N, Z=7).

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Using the data in the table, what is the average atomic mass of boron?

choices.explanation: 10.5 is the simple average of 10 and 11 without weighting by abundance. The weighted average must account for the 80.1% abundance of B-11.
Weighted average = $(0.199)(10.012) + (0.801)(11.009) = 1.992 + 8.818 = 10.81\ \text{u} \approx 10.8\ \text{u}$. The higher abundance of B-11 pulls the average closer to 11.
11.0 u would be correct only if B-11 had 100% abundance. With 19.9% B-10 contributing, the average falls below 11.
10.0 u would result if only B-10 were considered. Both isotopes contribute: $(0.199)(10.012) + (0.801)(11.009) \approx 10.8$ u.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Coulomb's law states that electrostatic force is proportional to $\frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$. Which of the following best explains why effective nuclear charge increases across Period 2?

choices.text: The number of core electrons increases, reducing shielding
choices.explanation: Core electrons in Period 2 remain at 2 (the 1s² electrons). They do not increase across the period. The change is in nuclear charge, not core electrons.

choices.text: The nuclear charge increases while the number of core shielding electrons remains approximately constant
choices.explanation: Across Period 2, each element gains one proton (+1 to nuclear charge). The 1s² core electrons (2 electrons) stay the same throughout. With more protons but similar shielding, effective nuclear charge ($Z_{eff}$) increases across the period.

choices.text: The atomic radius increases, reducing the Coulombic attraction
choices.explanation: Atomic radius decreases across a period — it does not increase. The increasing effective nuclear charge pulls electrons closer, shrinking the atom.

choices.text: Valence electrons shield each other completely from the nucleus
choices.explanation: Valence electrons in the same shell shield each other poorly (compared to core electrons). If they completely shielded, effective nuclear charge would not increase across the period.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following changes results in formation of an ion with the same electron configuration as a noble gas?

choices.explanation: Ca has configuration $[Ar]4s^2$. Losing 2 electrons gives $Ca^{2+}$ with configuration $[Ar]$ — identical to argon, a noble gas configuration.

Sulfur losing 2 electrons gives $\text{S}^{2+}$ with configuration $[\text{Ne}]3s^2 3p^2$, which has 14 electrons — not a noble gas configuration. Sulfur more commonly gains 2 electrons to form $\text{S}^{2-}$ with a noble gas configuration.

Nitrogen losing 3 electrons gives $N^{3+}$ with configuration $1s^2 2s^2$ (only 4 electrons) — this matches helium structure if we consider 2 electrons, but not a standard noble gas configuration. Nitrogen typically gains electrons, not loses them.
Oxygen losing 2 electrons gives $O^{2+}$ with configuration $1s^2 2s^2 2p^2$ — this is a carbon-like configuration, not a noble gas.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: A compound of carbon and hydrogen contains 85.7% C and 14.3% H by mass. The molar mass is determined to be 56 g/mol. What is the molecular formula?

choices.explanation: $CH_2$ is the empirical formula (molar mass = 14 g/mol). Since 56/14 = 4, the molecular formula is 4 times the empirical formula.
$C_2H_4$ has molar mass = 28 g/mol. Since the actual molar mass is 56 g/mol and 56/28 = 2, the molecular formula should be twice $C_2H_4 = C_4H_8$, not $C_2H_4$.
Empirical: C = 85.7/12 = 7.14; H = 14.3/1 = 14.3. Ratio: C:H = 7.14:14.3 = 1:2, so empirical formula = $CH_2$ (MW = 14). Ratio = 56/14 = 4. Molecular formula = $(CH_2)_4 = C_4H_8$.
$C_3H_6$ has molar mass = 42 g/mol. The actual molar mass is 56 g/mol. $C_3H_6$ does not match the given molar mass.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following subatomic particles primarily determines an atom's chemical properties?

choices.text: Neutrons, because they stabilize the nucleus
choices.explanation: Neutrons contribute to nuclear stability and mass number but do not significantly affect chemical behavior, which depends on electron configuration.

choices.text: Protons, because they determine the atomic number
choices.explanation: While protons determine atomic number (and thus the element), the chemical properties are directly determined by electron configuration, which is how atoms interact with other atoms.

choices.text: Electrons, because they are involved in bond formation and chemical reactions
choices.explanation: Chemical reactions involve making and breaking bonds, which requires electrons. The number and arrangement of electrons (especially valence electrons) determine how atoms interact, form bonds, and behave chemically.

choices.text: Quarks, because they make up protons and neutrons
choices.explanation: Quarks are subcomponents of protons and neutrons at the nuclear physics level. They do not participate in chemical reactions, which occur at the electron/bond level.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Going down Group 2 (alkaline earth metals) from Be to Ba, which of the following trends is correct?

choices.text: Atomic radius decreases; ionization energy increases
choices.explanation: Going down a group, atomic radius increases (more electron shells are added). Ionization energy decreases because the outer electrons are farther from the nucleus and better shielded.

choices.text: Atomic radius increases; ionization energy decreases
choices.explanation: Going down a group: each element adds a new electron shell (larger radius) and the outer electrons are farther from the nucleus with more shielding (lower ionization energy).

choices.text: Atomic radius increases; ionization energy increases
choices.explanation: Larger atoms with outer electrons farther from the nucleus and better shielded require less energy to ionize — ionization energy decreases going down a group.

choices.text: Atomic radius decreases; ionization energy decreases
choices.explanation: Atomic radius increases going down a group because each element has more electron shells. The premise of decreasing radius is incorrect.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A researcher measures the photoelectron spectrum of an unknown element. The spectrum shows four distinct peaks at binding energies of approximately 2800 eV, 130 eV, 20 eV, and 5 eV. The relative heights of the peaks correspond to 2, 2, 6, and 2 electrons respectively.

questions.question: Based on the photoelectron spectrum described, how many valence electrons does the unknown element have, and what is its most likely identity?

choices.text: 2 valence electrons; the element is beryllium (Be)
choices.explanation: Beryllium has only 4 electrons total with two peaks (2 and 2). The description shows 4 peaks totaling 2+2+6+2=12 electrons, placing it in Period 3.

choices.text: 8 valence electrons; the element is neon (Ne)
choices.explanation: Neon has 10 electrons with configuration $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$ and only 3 peaks. The spectrum described has 4 peaks with 12 total electrons.

choices.text: 2 valence electrons; the element is magnesium (Mg)
choices.explanation: Total electrons = 2+2+6+2 = 12 = magnesium (Z=12). The lowest binding energy peak (5 eV, 2 electrons) represents the $3s^2$ valence electrons. The higher-energy peaks represent $1s^2$, $2s^2$, and $2p^6$ core electrons.

choices.text: 6 valence electrons; the element is sulfur (S)
choices.explanation: Sulfur has 16 electrons. The described spectrum has 2+2+6+2=12 electrons total — not 16. The element is magnesium.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following sequences correctly ranks the species $Na^+$, $Mg^{2+}$, and $Al^{3+}$ in order of increasing ionic radius?

choices.explanation: This is the opposite of the correct order. All three are isoelectronic (10 electrons each), and higher nuclear charge means smaller ionic radius.

All three ions are isoelectronic (same electron configuration, 10 electrons). More protons in the nucleus with the same electron count means stronger attraction and smaller radius. Al³⁺ (Z=13) < Mg²⁺ (Z=12) < Na⁺ (Z=11).
For isoelectronic species, more protons = smaller radius. Al³⁺ (Z=13) is smallest; Na⁺ (Z=11) is largest. This ordering is incorrect.
This ordering places Mg²⁺ as largest, but Na⁺ should be largest among these isoelectronic ions (fewest protons per electron). Correct order: Al³⁺ < Mg²⁺ < Na⁺.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: A chemist has 3.01 × 10²³ atoms of gold (Au, molar mass = 197 g/mol). What is the mass of this sample?

choices.explanation: 197 g corresponds to 1 mole ($6.022 \times 10^{23}$ atoms). The sample has $3.01 \times 10^{23}$ atoms = 0.5 mole, which has half the mass.
59.2 g ≈ 197/3.33 — this does not correspond to the correct calculation. 0.5 mol Au × 197 g/mol = 98.5 g.
394 g = 2 × 197 g (2 moles). The sample has only half a mole ($3.01 \times 10^{23}$ atoms), so the mass is 0.5 × 197 = 98.5 g.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: The successive ionization energies of an unknown element X are: IE₁ = 590, IE₂ = 1145, IE₃ = 4912, IE₄ = 6491 kJ/mol. In which group of the periodic table is element X most likely found?

choices.explanation: Group 1 elements would show a dramatic jump between IE₁ and IE₂. The data shows IE₁ and IE₂ are relatively similar (590 to 1145 — about doubling), but the large jump is between IE₂ and IE₃.

The large jump occurs between IE₂ and IE₃ (1145 to 4912 kJ/mol — a 4-fold increase). This indicates 2 valence electrons — after removing both valence electrons, the third must come from a core shell, requiring dramatically more energy. Element X is in Group 2.
Group 3 would show the large jump between IE₃ and IE₄. The data shows the large jump occurs between IE₂ and IE₃, indicating only 2 valence electrons.
Group 4 would show the dramatic jump between IE₄ and IE₅. The data shows it between IE₂ and IE₃, indicating 2 valence electrons (Group 2).

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Element X has atomic number 35. How many core electrons does element X have?

choices.explanation: 7 is the number of valence electrons in bromine. Core electrons are all electrons NOT in the outermost shell.

Bromine (Z=35) has configuration $[Ar]3d^{10}4s^2 4p^5$. Valence electrons (n=4): $4s^2 4p^5$ = 7. Core electrons = 35 − 7 = 28. Note: $[Ar]$ = 18 electrons + $3d^{10}$ = 10 more = 28 total core electrons.
18 core electrons would be the [Ar] core only, ignoring the $3d^{10}$ electrons that are also core (not in the n=4 valence shell). Total core = 28.
35 is the total number of electrons (atomic number). Core electrons exclude the valence shell (n=4): 35 − 7 valence = 28 core.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Based on the data in the table, which of the following best explains the trend in first ionization energy going down Group 1?

choices.text: Increasing nuclear charge pulls valence electrons closer, making them harder to remove
choices.explanation: While nuclear charge does increase going down Group 1, the addition of new electron shells increases atomic radius and shielding far more than the nuclear charge increase. The net effect is decreasing ionization energy.

choices.text: Increasing atomic radius and shielding reduce the effective nuclear charge felt by valence electrons
choices.explanation: Going down Group 1, each element adds a new electron shell (radius increases: 152→186→227→248 pm). More core electrons shield the nucleus. The valence electron is farther from the nucleus and held less tightly, requiring less energy to remove (ionization energy decreases: 520→496→419→403 kJ/mol).

choices.text: The valence electrons in heavier alkali metals have higher energy due to greater electron-electron repulsion
choices.explanation: Electron-electron repulsion among core electrons does contribute to shielding, but the primary explanation is increasing atomic radius and effective shielding, not repulsion between the single valence electrons of different Group 1 elements.

choices.text: Heavier alkali metals have fewer valence electrons, making ionization easier
choices.explanation: All Group 1 metals have exactly one valence electron (ns¹). The number of valence electrons does not change going down the group. The decrease in ionization energy is due to increasing atomic radius and shielding.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: An atom of carbon-14 ($^{14}C$) has how many neutrons?

choices.explanation: 6 is the atomic number of carbon — the number of protons. Neutrons = mass number − atomic number = 14 − 6 = 8.
For carbon-14: atomic number (Z) = 6 protons; mass number (A) = 14. Neutrons = A − Z = 14 − 6 = 8 neutrons.
14 is the mass number, which equals protons + neutrons. Subtracting the 6 protons gives 14 − 6 = 8 neutrons.
20 neutrons would give a mass number of 6 + 20 = 26, making this carbon-26 — not carbon-14. The correct calculation is 14 − 6 = 8 neutrons.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A photoelectron spectrum of element X shows peaks with relative areas in the ratio 2:2:6:2:6:1. The peaks appear at binding energies from highest to lowest.

questions.question: Based on the photoelectron spectrum described, what is the identity of element X?

choices.explanation: The sum 2+2+6+2+6+1 = 19 electrons, not 15. Phosphorus would show 2+2+6+2+3 = 15 electrons.
Sulfur has 16 electrons: 2+2+6+2+4 = 16. The spectrum described totals 2+2+6+2+6+1 = 19 electrons.

Total electrons = 2+2+6+2+6+1 = 19. This matches potassium (K, Z=19), with configuration $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 4s^1$. Each peak corresponds to a subshell, with the last peak (1 electron) being the single $4s^1$ valence electron.
Calcium has 20 electrons: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 4s^2$ — the last peak would show 2 electrons, not 1. The described spectrum (last peak = 1 electron) matches potassium.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: The table lists isoelectronic species (all with 10 electrons). Which of the following correctly predicts the order of increasing ionic/atomic radius?

choices.explanation: This is the reverse of the correct order. More protons pulling the same 10 electrons inward means smaller radius. The species with the most protons (Mg²⁺) has the smallest radius.

All species have 10 electrons. More protons = greater nuclear attraction = smaller radius. Mg²⁺ (12 protons) is smallest; O²⁻ (8 protons) is largest. As proton count decreases from 12 to 8, radius increases: Mg²⁺ < Na⁺ < Ne < F⁻ < O²⁻.
Ne has 10 protons and is not the smallest. Mg²⁺ has 12 protons — the most — making it the smallest. The correct order by increasing radius is Mg²⁺ < Na⁺ < Ne < F⁻ < O²⁻.
This ordering is incorrect. Species with fewer protons (like O²⁻ with 8) have less nuclear attraction for the same 10 electrons and are therefore larger, not smaller.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following electron configurations represents an excited state of a sodium atom (Z = 11)?

choices.explanation: This is the correct ground-state configuration of sodium. It follows the Aufbau principle with electrons filling in order of increasing energy.

This configuration has 11 electrons (like sodium) but places the last electron in 3p instead of the lower-energy 3s. Since 3s < 3p in energy, having an electron in 3p without filling 3s first represents an excited state.

This configuration has 11 electrons but represents an excited state of a different type — the 2p has lost an electron. However, this is actually also an excited state, not ground state. But option B more clearly represents a standard excited state for Na.
This configuration has 12 electrons, which represents magnesium — not an excited sodium atom. Sodium has 11 electrons.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A compound contains only carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen. Combustion analysis of a 1.500 g sample produces 2.200 g of CO₂ and 0.900 g of H₂O.

questions.question: Which of the following best represents the empirical formula of this compound?

choices.explanation: $C_2H_4O_2$ is a molecular formula with the same 1:2:1 ratio, but it represents the molecular formula, not the empirical formula. The empirical (simplest) formula is $CH_2O$.
$CH_4O$ would require a C:H:O ratio of 1:4:1. From combustion analysis, the H ratio is 2 (not 4) per carbon. Recalculating: 0.050 mol C, 0.100 mol H, 0.050 mol O gives 1:2:1 = $CH_2O$.
A 2:2:1 ratio would require twice as many carbons as oxygens but equal C and H. The data gives equal moles of C and O (both 0.0500 mol) with double the H — that's a 1:2:1 ratio, not 2:2:1.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Based on the successive ionization energies in the table, in which group of the periodic table would this element most likely be found?

choices.explanation: A Group 1 element would show a dramatic jump after IE₁ (removing the one valence electron). The data shows three relatively close values (IE₁ to IE₃) before the large jump at IE₄.
A Group 2 element shows the large jump between IE₂ and IE₃. The data shows IE₁ = 800, IE₂ = 2400, IE₃ = 3700 are in the same order of magnitude; the enormous jump occurs between IE₃ and IE₄.

The large jump occurs between IE₃ (3700 kJ/mol) and IE₄ (25000 kJ/mol) — nearly a 7-fold increase. This indicates 3 valence electrons are removed easily before hitting the core. Group 3 elements have 3 valence electrons.
A Group 4 element would show the large jump between IE₄ and IE₅. The data clearly shows the dramatic jump between IE₃ and IE₄, indicating the element has 3 valence electrons (Group 3).

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following best describes isotopes of the same element?

choices.text: Atoms with the same mass number but different atomic numbers
choices.explanation: Atoms with the same mass number but different atomic numbers are isobars, not isotopes. Isotopes have the same atomic number (same element) but different mass numbers.

choices.text: Atoms with the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons
choices.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same number of protons, same atomic number) that differ in the number of neutrons. This gives them different mass numbers but identical chemical behavior.

choices.text: Atoms with the same number of electrons but different charges
choices.explanation: Atoms with the same electrons but different charges would be ions (isoelectronic species). Isotopes are neutral atoms of the same element with different neutron counts.

choices.text: Atoms with different numbers of protons and the same number of neutrons
choices.explanation: Atoms with different numbers of protons are different elements — not isotopes. Isotopes of an element always have the same number of protons (same atomic number).

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A photoelectron spectrum shows two peaks. The peak at a higher binding energy (approximately 870 eV) corresponds to 2 electrons, and the peak at a lower binding energy (approximately 50 eV) corresponds to 2 electrons.

questions.question: Which element is most likely being analyzed in this photoelectron spectrum?

choices.explanation: Helium has only 2 electrons total and would show a single peak (1s²). The described spectrum shows two peaks with 4 total electrons.

Beryllium has 4 electrons with configuration $1s^2 2s^2$. This produces two peaks: one at high binding energy for the core $1s^2$ electrons and one at lower binding energy for the valence $2s^2$ electrons. Total = 2 + 2 = 4 electrons.
Carbon has 6 electrons: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^2$. This would produce three peaks (1s, 2s, 2p) with 2, 2, and 2 electrons respectively. The described spectrum shows only two peaks.
Neon has 10 electrons: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$ — three peaks with 2, 2, and 6 electrons. The described spectrum shows only two peaks with 2 electrons each (total 4), which matches beryllium.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following correctly explains why the electronegativity of fluorine (F) is greater than that of chlorine (Cl), even though both are in Group 17?

choices.text: Fluorine has fewer electrons, so there is less electron-electron repulsion
choices.explanation: While fluorine does have fewer electrons, electronegativity differences are primarily explained by atomic size and effective nuclear charge, not by electron-electron repulsion in the atom being analyzed.

choices.text: Fluorine has a smaller atomic radius, so the nucleus attracts shared electrons more strongly
choices.explanation: Fluorine (Period 2) is much smaller than chlorine (Period 3). With a smaller atomic radius, the fluorine nucleus is closer to the shared electrons in a bond, exerting a stronger attractive force — resulting in the highest electronegativity of all elements.

choices.text: Fluorine has a higher ionization energy than chlorine, so it holds electrons more tightly in all situations
choices.explanation: While fluorine does have a higher ionization energy, this measures how tightly an atom holds its own electrons, not necessarily its ability to attract shared electrons from another atom. The best explanation for electronegativity involves atomic radius and nuclear proximity to bonding electrons.

choices.text: Chlorine is in Period 3, so its 3d orbitals reduce its attraction for bonding electrons
choices.explanation: Chlorine does not use 3d orbitals in typical covalent bonding. The difference in electronegativity is due to atomic size: F is smaller, bringing its nucleus closer to shared electrons in a bond.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Elements X and Y in the table both deviate from the expected Aufbau electron configuration. Which of the following best explains this deviation?

choices.text: Extra stability is achieved by having a fully filled or half-filled d subshell
choices.explanation: Cr (Z=24) achieves a stable half-filled $3d^5$ with $[Ar]3d^5 4s^1$ (expected $3d^4 4s^2$). Cu (Z=29) achieves a stable fully filled $3d^{10}$ with $[Ar]3d^{10} 4s^1$ (expected $3d^9 4s^2$). Half-filled and fully filled d subshells have extra exchange energy stability.

choices.text: The 4s subshell is higher in energy than the 3d subshell for these atoms
choices.explanation: In transition metals, the 3d and 4s orbitals are very close in energy. The deviation is not because 4s is simply higher in energy but because promoting one electron achieves special stability from a half-filled or fully filled d subshell.

choices.text: Electron-electron repulsion forces one electron from the 3d to the 4s subshell
choices.explanation: If repulsion were the cause, the electron would move away from the more crowded 3d, but the deviation actually moves an electron INTO the 3d (for Cr) or keeps a full 3d. The explanation is stability, not repulsion.

choices.text: The Pauli exclusion principle prevents electrons from pairing in the 3d subshell
choices.explanation: The Pauli exclusion principle states no two electrons can have identical quantum numbers — it doesn't cause this deviation. The anomaly is due to extra stability of half-filled and fully filled d subshells.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: The molar mass of $Ca_3(PO_4)_2$ is closest to which of the following values? (Ca = 40, P = 31, O = 16)

choices.explanation: $Ca_3(PO_4)_2$ has 3 Ca + 2 P + 8 O. Molar mass = 3(40) + 2(31) + 8(16) = 120 + 62 + 128 = 310 g/mol. 150 g/mol is far too low.
Molar mass = 3(40) + 2(31) + 8(16) = 120 + 62 + 128 = 310 g/mol. 250 g/mol would require fewer atoms than the formula indicates.
370 g/mol would require additional atoms not present in $Ca_3(PO_4)_2$. The correct calculation gives 310 g/mol: 3(40) + 2(31) + 8(16) = 120 + 62 + 128 = 310.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: Experiment A: Light of wavelength 300 nm is shone on metal X. No electrons are emitted. Experiment B: Light of wavelength 200 nm is shone on metal X. Electrons are emitted with kinetic energy of 2.5 eV.

questions.question: Based on the photoelectric effect experiments described, which of the following can be concluded about metal X?

choices.text: The work function of metal X is greater than the photon energy of 200 nm light
choices.explanation: If the work function were greater than 200 nm photon energy, no electrons would be emitted in Experiment B. Since electrons ARE emitted at 200 nm, the work function must be less than the energy of 200 nm photons.

choices.text: The work function of metal X lies between the photon energies of 300 nm and 200 nm light
choices.explanation: 300 nm photons do not eject electrons (photon energy < work function). 200 nm photons do eject electrons (photon energy > work function). Therefore, the work function lies between the energies corresponding to 300 nm and 200 nm light. Since E = hc/λ, shorter wavelength = higher energy, so work function is between these energies.

choices.text: Increasing the intensity of 300 nm light would eventually eject electrons from metal X
choices.explanation: This contradicts the photoelectric effect. If individual photons lack sufficient energy to exceed the work function, no amount of increased intensity will eject electrons. Photoelectric effect requires each photon to have energy ≥ work function.

choices.text: The emitted electrons from Experiment B have wavelength 200 nm
choices.explanation: The 200 nm refers to the wavelength of the incident light, not the emitted electrons. The emitted electrons have de Broglie wavelength based on their kinetic energy (2.5 eV), which is entirely different.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following pairs of elements would have the most similar chemical properties?

choices.explanation: Carbon and silicon are in the same group (Group 14) and thus have the same number of valence electrons ($ns^2 np^2$). Elements in the same group have similar valence electron configurations and therefore similar chemical properties.

Carbon (Group 14, 4 valence electrons) and nitrogen (Group 15, 5 valence electrons) are in the same period but different groups. They have different valence electron configurations and different chemical properties.
Na (Group 1, 1 valence electron) and Mg (Group 2, 2 valence electrons) are in different groups and have different chemical properties — Na forms 1+ ions, Mg forms 2+ ions.

Li (Group 1, 1 valence electron) and C (Group 14, 4 valence electrons) are in different groups and have very different chemical properties. Same group membership is the key indicator of similar chemical behavior.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following best explains why the 4s subshell fills before the 3d subshell in transition metal electron configurations?

choices.text: The 4s orbital has a higher principal quantum number, so it has more energy
choices.explanation: A higher principal quantum number generally means higher energy, but for transition metals, the 4s subshell is lower in energy than 3d due to the penetration effect of s orbitals toward the nucleus.

choices.text: The 4s orbital penetrates closer to the nucleus than 3d orbitals, lowering its energy below 3d
choices.explanation: The 4s orbital has radial nodes that allow it to penetrate the electron density of core shells and spend time close to the nucleus. This penetration lowers the energy of 4s below that of 3d in the neutral atom context of filling, so 4s fills first per the Aufbau principle.

choices.text: The 3d subshell has a higher angular momentum quantum number (l=2), which always means higher energy
choices.explanation: While higher l generally means higher energy within the same shell, the comparison between 4s (n=4, l=0) and 3d (n=3, l=2) involves different shells, making the penetration effect of 4s decisive in placing it lower in energy.

choices.text: The 4s has fewer orbitals than 3d, making it easier to fill
choices.explanation: The order of filling is determined by energy, not the number of orbitals. The 4s has 1 orbital (capacity 2 electrons) and 3d has 5 orbitals (capacity 10 electrons), but filling order depends solely on relative energy levels.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A gaseous compound contains only nitrogen and oxygen. Analysis shows the compound is 30.4% nitrogen and 69.6% oxygen by mass.

questions.question: What is the empirical formula of this compound?

choices.explanation: $NO$ has 46.7% N and 53.3% O — this doesn't match 30.4% N and 69.6% O. The data gives a different N:O ratio.
In 100 g: N = 30.4 g / 14 g·mol⁻¹ = 2.17 mol; O = 69.6 g / 16 g·mol⁻¹ = 4.35 mol. Ratio N:O = 2.17:4.35 = 1:2. Empirical formula = $NO_2$.
$N_2O$ has 63.7% N and 36.4% O — the opposite ratio from the data. $N_2O$ is nitrogen-rich, but this compound is oxygen-rich at 69.6% O.
$N_2O_3$ has the same 1:1.5 ratio as $NO_{1.5}$. Check: 2N = 28, 3O = 48, MW = 76. %N = 28/76 = 36.8%, %O = 63.2%. This doesn't match 30.4% N. The correct formula is $NO_2$ (N:O = 1:2).

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following best predicts the relative atomic radii of $Na$, $Mg$, and $Al$ (all in Period 3)?

choices.explanation: This is the opposite of the correct trend. Across a period, increasing nuclear charge (with the same electron shell) pulls electrons inward, decreasing atomic radius. Na is the largest.

Across Period 3, nuclear charge increases (Na: Z=11, Mg: Z=12, Al: Z=13) while all three have electrons in the n=3 shell. Greater nuclear charge contracts the electron cloud, so atomic radius decreases: Na > Mg > Al, or Al < Mg < Na.
This ordering has Mg as the smallest but Al as the largest — inconsistent with the periodic trend of decreasing radius across a period due to increasing effective nuclear charge.
This ordering has Na as the smallest, which contradicts the periodic trend. Na (Z=11) has the lowest nuclear charge among the three and the largest atomic radius.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: How many total electrons are in a sulfate ion ($SO_4^{2-}$)?

choices.explanation: This counts S (16) + 4×O (4×8=32) = 48, but ignores the 2− charge. A 2− ion has 2 extra electrons: 48 + 2 = 50.
S (Z=16): 16 electrons; 4 oxygen atoms (Z=8 each): 4×8=32 electrons. Total from atoms = 48. Plus 2 extra electrons from the 2− charge = 50 electrons total.
46 would result from losing 2 electrons (2+ charge), not gaining 2. The sulfate ion has a 2− charge, meaning 2 extra electrons have been added: 48 + 2 = 50.
32 counts only the 4 oxygen atoms (4×8=32), omitting sulfur's 16 electrons and the 2 extra electrons from the charge. Total = 16 + 32 + 2 = 50.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student uses a mass spectrometer to analyze a sample of element X. The instrument detects two isotopes: one with mass 106.91 u at 51.84% abundance and another with mass 108.90 u at 48.16% abundance.

questions.question: Which of the following is the most likely identity of element X, and what is the best estimate of its average atomic mass?

choices.explanation: Average atomic mass = (0.5184)(106.91) + (0.4816)(108.90) = 55.41 + 52.45 = 107.86 ≈ 107.9 u. Silver (Ag, Z=47) has a standard atomic weight of 107.87 u with two natural isotopes Ag-107 and Ag-109, matching these data.

Palladium (Z=46) has six stable isotopes and a standard atomic mass of 106.42 u. The calculated average from the data is 107.9 u, and two isotopes at 51.84%/48.16% strongly match silver's isotope pattern.
Cadmium (Z=48) has 8 stable isotopes and average atomic mass of 112.4 u. The calculated value from the data (≈107.9 u) does not match cadmium's average atomic mass.
Rhodium (Z=45) is monoisotopic (only Rh-103) and has an atomic mass of 102.9 u. It would not show two peaks in a mass spectrum. The data with two isotopes near 107 and 109 u fits silver.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between the atomic number, mass number, and number of neutrons for an atom?

choices.text: Mass number = atomic number + number of electrons
choices.explanation: The mass number equals protons plus neutrons (not electrons). Electrons are too light to significantly contribute to atomic mass.

choices.text: Number of neutrons = mass number − atomic number
choices.explanation: Mass number (A) = protons (Z) + neutrons (N). Rearranging: N = A − Z. For example, $^{56}Fe$: neutrons = 56 − 26 = 30 neutrons.

choices.text: Atomic number = mass number + number of neutrons
choices.explanation: This equation is inverted. The correct relationship is: mass number = atomic number + neutrons. Therefore atomic number = mass number − neutrons, not mass number + neutrons.

choices.text: Mass number = number of neutrons + number of electrons
choices.explanation: Mass number = protons + neutrons. Electrons are not included in the mass number calculation. The mass of an electron is approximately 1/1836 the mass of a proton or neutron — negligible.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: Coulomb's law states that the force between two charges q₁ and q₂ separated by distance r is: $F = k\frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$. In chemistry, this law is used qualitatively to explain trends in atomic properties.

questions.question: According to Coulomb's law, which of the following best explains why the lattice energy of $MgO$ is much greater than that of $NaCl$?

choices.text: $MgO$ has larger ions than $NaCl$, increasing the distance between charges
choices.explanation: $Mg^{2+}$ and $O^{2-}$ are actually smaller than $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$ respectively. Greater distance would decrease lattice energy. $MgO$ has higher lattice energy due to higher charges, not larger size.

choices.text: $MgO$ has 2+ and 2− ions, so both the charge magnitude and charge product are larger than in $NaCl$'s 1+ and 1− ions
choices.explanation: Lattice energy $\propto \frac{|q_+||q_-|}{r}$. For $NaCl$: $q_+ \times q_- = 1 \times 1 = 1$. For $MgO$: $q_+ \times q_- = 2 \times 2 = 4$. The charge product is 4× larger, and $Mg^{2+}$/$O^{2-}$ are smaller (shorter r). Both effects give $MgO$ dramatically higher lattice energy.

choices.text: $MgO$ is a covalent compound, so electron sharing increases the binding force
choices.explanation: $MgO$ is an ionic compound, not covalent. The high lattice energy results from the strong electrostatic attraction between Mg²⁺ and O²⁻ ions, explained by Coulomb's law with 2+ and 2− charges.

choices.text: Magnesium is a heavier atom than sodium, contributing more nuclear mass to the lattice
choices.explanation: Nuclear mass does not determine lattice energy. Lattice energy is an electrostatic property governed by ionic charges and interionic distances (Coulomb's law), not atomic mass.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following statements about Hund's rule is correct?

choices.text: Electrons fill orbitals of lowest energy first before occupying higher energy orbitals
choices.explanation: This describes the Aufbau principle, not Hund's rule. Hund's rule specifically governs how electrons distribute among orbitals of equal energy (degenerate orbitals).

choices.text: No two electrons in an atom can have the same four quantum numbers
choices.explanation: This is the Pauli exclusion principle, not Hund's rule. Hund's rule addresses electron distribution in degenerate orbitals.

choices.text: When filling degenerate orbitals, electrons occupy each orbital singly before pairing
choices.explanation: Hund's rule states that when electrons fill orbitals of equal energy (degenerate orbitals, such as three 2p orbitals), one electron enters each orbital with parallel spins before any pairing occurs. This minimizes electron-electron repulsion.

choices.text: Electrons in the same orbital must have opposite spins
choices.explanation: While it's true that paired electrons must have opposite spins (a consequence of the Pauli exclusion principle), this statement describes Pauli's principle. Hund's rule addresses how electrons distribute across multiple degenerate orbitals.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: All three species in the table have the same electron configuration ($1s^2$). Which of the following correctly ranks their radii from smallest to largest?

choices.explanation: This incorrectly places Li⁺ as the largest. Li⁺ has Z=3 — the highest nuclear charge — which pulls electrons closest, making Li⁺ the smallest.

All three have 2 electrons in $1s^2$. Higher nuclear charge attracts electrons more strongly → smaller radius. Li⁺ (Z=3) is smallest; He (Z=2) is intermediate; H⁻ (Z=1) has the weakest nuclear attraction for these 2 electrons and is the largest.
H⁻ has only Z=1, so its 2 electrons experience the weakest nuclear attraction and occupy the largest volume. Li⁺ (Z=3) has the strongest attraction and is smallest.
He (Z=2) is intermediate in nuclear charge. The correct order by increasing radius based on decreasing nuclear charge is: Li⁺ (Z=3, smallest) < He (Z=2) < H⁻ (Z=1, largest).

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A chemist needs to calculate the percent by mass of oxygen in aspirin ($C_9H_8O_4$, molar mass = 180 g/mol).

questions.question: What is the percent by mass of oxygen in aspirin?

choices.explanation: This might result from miscounting oxygen atoms. Aspirin has 4 oxygen atoms: 4 × 16 = 64 g/mol of oxygen. Percent = (64/180) × 100 = 35.6%, not 17.8%.
26.7% could result from dividing the mass of 3 O atoms (48 g/mol) by 180 g/mol. Aspirin has 4 oxygen atoms: (4×16)/180 × 100 = 64/180 × 100 = 35.6%.
Mass of O in one mole of aspirin = 4 × 16 = 64 g/mol. Percent by mass = (64/180) × 100 = 35.6%. With 4 oxygen atoms contributing 64 out of 180 total g/mol.
44.4% would be the percentage if approximately 80 g/mol were oxygen. With 4 oxygen atoms: 4 × 16 = 64 g/mol, and 64/180 × 100 = 35.6%.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry

stimulus.content: A student observes that going from lithium to beryllium across Period 2, the first ionization energy increases as expected. However, going from beryllium (IE₁ = 900 kJ/mol) to boron (IE₁ = 800 kJ/mol), the first ionization energy unexpectedly decreases.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains the decrease in first ionization energy from beryllium to boron?

choices.text: Boron has more protons than beryllium, so electron repulsion is greater
choices.explanation: More protons would increase nuclear attraction and generally increase ionization energy. The anomaly arises from which subshell the removed electron comes from, not from proton count.

choices.text: Boron's valence electron is in a 2p orbital, which is higher in energy and further from the nucleus than beryllium's 2s orbital
choices.explanation: Beryllium: $[He]2s^2$ — the highest-energy electron is in 2s. Boron: $[He]2s^2 2p^1$ — the highest-energy electron is in the 2p subshell, which is higher in energy and farther from the nucleus than 2s. The 2p electron is easier to remove despite boron's higher nuclear charge.

choices.text: Boron has fewer electrons than beryllium, so there is less electron-electron repulsion
choices.explanation: Boron (Z=5) has more electrons (5) than beryllium (Z=4, 4 electrons). This answer reverses the electron count and misidentifies the cause of the ionization energy anomaly.

choices.text: Beryllium's 2s subshell is half-filled, giving it exceptional stability
choices.explanation: A half-filled subshell applies to p, d, or f subshells (where each orbital has exactly one electron). Beryllium's $2s^2$ is a fully filled s subshell, which does have extra stability — but the correct explanation focuses on boron's electron being in the higher-energy 2p orbital.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following electron configurations represents the ion $Fe^{3+}$?

choices.explanation: This is the ground-state configuration of neutral iron (Fe, Z=26). $Fe^{3+}$ has lost 3 electrons from neutral Fe.
This has 6 electrons beyond [Ar], giving Fe⁺ (lost only 1 electron). Also, when Fe loses electrons, it loses from 4s before 3d: $Fe^+ = [Ar]3d^6 4s^1$.

Neutral Fe: $[Ar]3d^6 4s^2$. When forming $Fe^{3+}$, electrons are removed from 4s first, then 3d: lose both 4s electrons, then one 3d electron: $[Ar]3d^5$. This is also a half-filled d subshell (extra stability).

This configuration has 5 electrons beyond [Ar] — it represents $Fe^{2+}$, not $Fe^{3+}$. Furthermore, when transition metals ionize, 4s electrons are removed before 3d electrons, so $Fe^{2+}$ is $[Ar]3d^6$, not $[Ar]3d^4 4s^1$.

questions.subject: ap-chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following represents the correct orbital notation for the valence electrons of carbon (Z = 6)?

choices.text: Both 2p electrons are paired in one 2p orbital (↑↓ in one orbital, two empty)
choices.explanation: This violates Hund's rule. Electrons in degenerate orbitals distribute to maximize unpaired spins before pairing. Carbon's two 2p electrons each occupy separate 2p orbitals.

choices.text: One 2p electron with spin up in one orbital, one 2p electron with spin up in another orbital (two unpaired electrons)
choices.explanation: Hund's rule requires electrons to occupy degenerate orbitals singly before pairing. Carbon ($1s^2 2s^2 2p^2$) has two 2p electrons, each in a separate 2p orbital with parallel (same) spins: ↑ _ ↑ _.

choices.text: All three 2p orbitals contain one electron each (three unpaired electrons)
choices.explanation: Three unpaired 2p electrons would require Z=7 (nitrogen, $2p^3$). Carbon only has 2 electrons in its 2p subshell.

choices.text: One electron with spin up, one with spin down in the same 2p orbital, one empty 2p orbital
choices.explanation: This shows a paired orbital (violating Hund's rule). Two electrons in the same orbital would experience extra repulsion. Hund's rule dictates they should be in separate 2p orbitals with parallel spins.
